query: enzyme common to glycogenesis and glycogenolysis

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogenolysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen, also known as animal starch, is a branched polysaccharide that serves as an energy reserve in the liver and muscle. It is readily available as an immediate source of energy. The formation of glycogen from glucose is called glycogenesis, and the breakdown of glycogen to form glucose is called glycogen metabolism or glycogenolysis. Increased cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) produces the breakdown of glycogen (glycogenolysis). Various hormones such as insulin, glucagon, cortisol, and others regulate the relationship between glycogenesis and gluconeogenesis. Glycogenolysis is initiated by the action of a specific enzyme called phosphorylase, which yields glucose-1-phosphate. Glucose-1-phosphate is an important compound at the intersection of several metabolic pathways, such as glycolysis, glycogenesis, glycogenolysis, and gluconeogenesis. Interestingly, when glycogenesis occurs, glycogenolysis is inhibited, and vice versa. Impaired glycogen metabolism is associated with various inherited metabolic disorders collectively known as glycogen storage diseases. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen, also known as animal starch, is a branched polysaccharide that serves as a reserve of carbohydrates in the body; it is stored in the liver and muscle and readily available as an immediate energy source. The formation of glycogen from glucose is known as glycogenesis, and the breakdown of glycogen to form glucose is called glycogen metabolism or glycogenolysis. Increased cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) catalyzes the breakdown of glycogen (glycogenolysis). The primary hormones that regulate the cycle of glycogenesis and gluconeogenesis are insulin, glucagon, and cortisol. Glycogenolysis is initiated by the action of a specific enzyme called phosphorylase, which yields glucose-1-phosphate (P). Glucose-1-P is an essential compound at the intersection of several metabolic pathways, such as glycolysis, glycogenesis, glycogenolysis, and gluconeogenesis. When glycogenesis occurs, glycogenolysis is inhibited, and vice versa. Impaired glycogen metabolism, characterized by abnormal glycogen accumulation, is associated with inherited metabolic disorders and is collectively known as glycogen storage diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogenolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The key regulatory enzymes of glycogenolysis are phosphorylase kinase and glycogen phosphorylase, both activated by phosphorylation. These are predominantly expressed in the liver, muscle, and brain. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Molecular Level -- Steps of Glycogenolysis
retrieved_docs.content: In muscles, the enzyme glucose-6-phosphatase is absent. Therefore, glucose-6-P enters into the glycolytic cycle and forms pyruvate and lactic acid. But indirectly, lactic acid can serve as an intermediate molecule for glucose formation in the liver. Muscle glycogen cannot provide blood glucose by glycogenolysis due to a lack of the enzyme glucose-6-phosphatase. It is important to note that besides phosphorolytic cleavage, 2 additional pathways for glycogenolysis have been discovered, involving α-amylase (α-amylolysis) and γ-amylase (γ-amylolysis). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogenolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The process of glycogenolysis starts in the muscle due to the activity of the enzyme adenyl cyclase and cAMP. cAMP then binds to phosphorylase kinase and converts it to its active form, which then converts phosphorylase b to phosphorylase a, which finally catalyzes the breakdown of glycogen. [9] The process of glycogen breakdown can occur either in the cytosol or in the lysosomes. In the cytosol, the glycogen phosphorylase catalyzes the nonreducing ends of glycogen branches, releasing glucose-1-phosphate. Its action stops 4 glucose residues before an α1→6 junction. Glycogen phosphorylase can only cleave alpha 1,4 glycosidic bonds in glycogen; it cannot cleave the alpha 1,6 glycosidic bonds, which make up the branching points.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen storage disease Type V, McArdle disease, develops due to a skeletal muscle glycogen phosphorylase deficiency. Patients display exercise intolerance, muscle weakness, cramping, and pain. Creatine kinase levels are elevated, and myoglobinuria can also develop. A typical symptom of McArdle disease is second wind, where patients can resume exercise when resting briefly. Ingestion of sucrose before exercise can help alleviate symptoms since this becomes the energy source during exercise before resorting to glycogen stores.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The enzymes glycogen synthase and glycogen phosphorylase regulate the balance of glycogen metabolism. Glycogenesis responds to hormonal control and second messenger signals. One of the primary forms of control is the varied phosphorylation of glycogen synthase and glycogen phosphorylase. Enzymes under hormonal control regulate phosphorylation, and there are many different effectors compared to allosteric regulation systems.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease -- Introduction -- Classification of Glycogen Storage Disorder
retrieved_docs.content: GSDs that primarily affect the liver include the following: Glycogen synthase-2 deficiency (GSD type 0a) Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency (GSD type Ia) Glucose-6-phosphate transporter deficiency (GSD type Ib) Glycogen debrancher deficiency (GSD type III) Glycogen branching enzyme deficiency (GSD type IV) Liver phosphorylase deficiency (GSD type VI) Phosphorylase kinase deficiency (GSD type IXa) GLUT2 deficiency or Fanconi-Bickel disease GSDs that primarily affect the skeletal muscles include the following: Muscle phosphorylase deficiency (GSD type V) Phosphofructokinase deficiency (GSD type VII) Phosphoglycerate mutase deficiency (GSD type X) Lactate dehydrogenase A deficiency (GSD type XI) Aldolase A deficiency (GSD type XII) β-enolase deficiency (GSD type XIII) Phosphoglucomutase-1 deficiency (GSD type XIV) GSDs that affect both skeletal and cardiac muscles include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: While glycogen degradation by phosphorylase and debranching enzyme can occur in the cytosol, glycogen is also degraded via a lysosomal pathway, leading to a lysosomal storage disease called Pompe disease (glycogen storage disease Type II). In Pompe disease, a mutation involving lysosomal alpha-glucosidase—also known as acid maltase—develops. As a result, glycogen accumulates in the lysosome and its vesicles, leading to fatal outcomes, including cardiomyopathy and muscular hypotonia. The mechanism of glycogen transportation to lysosomes is not fully understood, but it is believed to occur via macroautophagy. During this process, the cargo is engulfed within double-membrane vesicles known as autophagosomes that fuse with the lysosome. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When glycogen phosphorylase is deficient in the liver, a different disease develops—Type VI GSD. Type VI GSD, or Hers disease, exhibits normal creatine kinase and uric acid levels. Patients present with growth retardation and liver enlargement. Hyperlipidemia and ketotic hypoglycemia also are common. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry - Glycogenolysis -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Corresponding to cytosolic degradation, glycogen phosphorylase, the rate-limiting enzyme of glycogenolysis, cleaves terminal glucose residue connected to a glycogen branch while substituting a phosphoryl group for the α-1-4 bond. Four residues before an α-1-6 bond, corresponding to a branch, glycogen debranching enzyme catalyzes the transfer of 3 of the 4 remaining glucose residues to the end of another glycogen chain, which can be degraded by glycogen phosphorylase. In other words, the breakage of α-1-4 glycosidic bonds present in linear chains is catalyzed by glycogen phosphorylase, and the addition of the phosphate group to position 1 results in the production of glucose-1-phosphate.

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, muscle cramps, exercise intolerance, and weakness develop when these physiological functions are defective. Some disorders also affect the myocardial tissue and can lead to cardiomyopathy and cardiac conduction defects. [10] Failure to maintain glucose homeostasis triggers alternate pathways to meet metabolic demands. In GSD type 1, for example, failure of glycogenolysis in the liver results in increased lactic acid production, resulting in lactic acidosis due to the intracellular accumulation of glucose-6-phosphate, which stimulates the glycolytic pathway.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Glycogen -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: There are many glycogen storage diseases (GSD) caused by genetic mutations in enzymes directly involved in the anabolism and catabolism of glycogen.  They are generally inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern and often present in early childhood. The major GSDs and the enzymes perturbed are as follows [3] [11] : Type 0: muscle glycogen synthase (GYS1) or liver glycogen synthase (GYS2) Type Ia (Von Gierke disease): glycogen-6-phosphatase, specifically G6PC (removes the phosphate group from G6P) Type 1b (Von Gierke disease): glycogen-6-phosphatase, specifically G6PT (transports G6P into the endoplasmic reticulum) Type II (Pompe disease): acid alpha-glucosidase (GAA; this enzyme is localized in the lysosome) Type III (Cori disease): glycogen debranching enzyme Type IV (Andersen disease): glycogen branching enzyme. Type V (McArdle disease): muscle and heart glycogen phosphorylase Type VI (Hers disease): liver glycogen phosphorylase

retrieved_docs.title: Glycogen Storage Disease -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogen synthase catalyzes the formation of α-1,4 glucose linkages in glycogen, but the glycogen branching enzyme ( GBE1 ) is required to create the branching α-1,6 linkages. Mutations in GBE1 lead to the production of abnormally structured glycogen, known as polyglucosan bodies, which is the hallmark of GSD type IV. These polyglucosan bodies accumulate in liver and muscle cells and do not effectively undergo glycogenolysis. In muscle tissue, this accumulation causes weakness and myopathy, while in the liver, it results in hepatomegaly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Glycogenolytic hormones are a class of hormones that stimulate the breakdown of glycogen into glucose, thereby increasing blood glucose levels. Pancreatic hormones, which are secreted by the pancreas, fall within this category because they include glucagon, a key glycogenolytic hormone that promotes glycogenolysis. This relationship highlights the role of pancreatic endocrine function in regulating glucose metabolism through glycogenolytic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1593
retrieved_docs.content: biosynthetic pathways related to protein and muscle growth. Finally, trisaccharides, as short carbohydrate chains, can serve as substrates or regulatory sugars that modulate glycosylation and other carbohydrate‑based biosynthetic processes. Together, these entities illustrate the wide range of chemical, pharmacologic, and biological factors that can define, regulate, or modify biosynthetic mechanisms, underscoring their importance for understanding metabolic regulation and for developing therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1501
retrieved_docs.content: Imiglucerase and laronidase are both classified as members of the enzyme group designated as Enzymes for Alimentary Tract and Metabolism. This categorization indicates that each protein functions as an enzyme involved in digestive or metabolic processes, and their inclusion in the same family highlights shared biochemical roles that are relevant for the treatment of metabolic storage disorders. Recognizing that both agents belong to this enzyme class assists clinicians in understanding their mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic applications within the alimentary and metabolic pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: The endokrinní systém (endocrine system) is the physiological network that regulates numerous metabolic processes and includes conditions such as متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) as a manifestation of its dysregulation. Within this system, pankreatické hormony (pancreatic hormones) represent a specific group of secretions that contribute to glucose homeostasis and other endocrine functions. Medical interventions that affect hormonal balance, such as kastrace (castration), are procedures that target the endokrinní systém, reflecting its role as a therapeutic focus in certain clinical contexts. Glycogenolytic hormones, which promote the breakdown of glycogen to glucose, constitute a distinct class of hormones that are not classified as pankreatické hormony, underscoring the diversity of hormonal actions within and beyond the endocrine system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) is a broad category of disease that includes carbohydrate metabolism disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات) as one of its subtypes. Within the hierarchy of carbohydrate metabolism disorder, other endocrine disorders are classified as a further subset, indicating that these endocrine conditions are considered specific forms of carbohydrate metabolism disorders. This hierarchical relationship highlights how metabolic disorders encompass a range of more specialized conditions, including those affecting carbohydrate processing and related endocrine functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 115
retrieved_docs.content: drugs—bile acids and their derivatives that support bile flow—and liver therapy agents such as lipotropics. Enzymes for Alimentary Tract and Metabolism, a subclass of OTHER ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM PRODUCTS, contains recombinant enzyme replacement therapies imiglucerase (for Gaucher disease) and laronidase (for mucopolysaccharidosis I). Together, this structured taxonomy links chemical families, mechanisms of action (pro‑kinetic, serotonergic blockade, anticholinergic, opioid antagonism, laxative, bile‑acid supplementation, enzyme replacement) with their therapeutic indications, facilitating precise prescribing and research in gastroenterology, hepatobiliary disorders, and metabolic enzyme deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 564
retrieved_docs.content: members of the enzyme subclass and are employed to replace deficient lysosomal enzymes in specific metabolic diseases. Overall, the ATC hierarchy illustrates how ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM DRUGS intersect with anabolic, bile‑related, and enzyme‑replacement therapies, and how these interrelated categories facilitate organized prescribing, research, and patient‑care strategies across a spectrum of digestive and metabolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 758
retrieved_docs.content: and their glycogenolytic subset forming a coherent hormonal hierarchy. This integrated structure aids clinicians in understanding procedural relationships, selecting appropriate surgical codes, and appreciating the distinct yet parallel organization of gynecologic and endocrine surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 954
retrieved_docs.content: Enzyme Replacements/Modifiers [TC] is a therapeutic class that comprises the enzymes alglucerase and hyaluronoglukosaminidasa, indicating that these agents are used to replace or modify deficient enzymatic activity in clinical practice. In parallel, the group Enzymes for ALIMENTARY TRACT AND METABOLISM includes alglucerase, imiglucerase, and laronidase, reflecting their functional roles in digestive and metabolic processes. Notably, alglucerase belongs to both categories, underscoring its dual significance as both an enzyme replacement/modifier and as an enzyme involved in alimentary tract and metabolic pathways. This overlap highlights the importance of recognizing the multiple therapeutic and physiological contexts in which a single enzyme may be applied, facilitating more comprehensive patient care and targeted treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad classification that encompasses several distinct conditions. Within this category, مرض السكري دون مضاعفات refers to diabetes without complications, representing a form of the disease where hyperglycemia is present but secondary complications have not yet developed. فرط أنسولين الدم denotes hyperinsulinemia, a state of elevated insulin levels in the blood that can arise from pancreatic beta‑cell overactivity or insulin‑secreting tumors and may lead to hypoglycemia. ورم غلوكاغوني describes glucagonoma, a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, causing characteristic metabolic disturbances such as glucose intolerance, weight loss, and a distinctive skin rash. Together, these three entities illustrate the spectrum of disorders that fall under DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES, highlighting the varied clinical presentations and management considerations associated with pancreatic endocrine pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: that glycogenolytic hormones represent a broader category that includes pancreatic hormones. Additionally, castration (kastrace) is identified as a procedure that targets the endocrine system. Clinically, the condition “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” has its findings located in the endocrine system, underscoring the endocrine system’s central role in gestational glucose‑metabolism disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1934
retrieved_docs.content: Nemoc is associated with اضطراب استقلاب الأحماض الأمينية, an amino‑acid metabolism disorder, indicating that individuals with nemoc may exhibit disturbances in amino‑acid metabolic pathways, which is relevant for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that encompasses several specific conditions. Among its subentities are اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), a chronic complication in which prolonged hyperglycemia leads to damage of peripheral nerves and results in sensory loss, pain, or autonomic dysfunction. The category also includes فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), a state of elevated insulin levels in the bloodstream that can arise from insulin resistance, early type 2 diabetes, or insulin‑secreting tumors and contributes to metabolic disturbances such as hypoglycemia and weight gain. Another child condition is ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma), a rare neoplasm of the pancreatic α‑cells that secretes excess glucagon, producing a characteristic syndrome of hyperglycemia, necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, and anemia. Finally, the category lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (progressive hemifacial atrophy), an uncommon, often autoimmune‑mediated disorder characterized by gradual wasting of the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle on one side of the face; its inclusion reflects the recognition that endocrine pancreatic diseases, particularly those with autoimmune components, may be associated with extra‑pancreatic manifestations. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical spectrum—from metabolic and neuropathic complications to neoplastic and autoimmune manifestations—linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and multidisciplinary management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2451
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is positioned within the larger classification of other endocrine disorders, which itself is nested under the broader endocrine condition described by نقص تركيز السكر (hypoglycemia). Within this disease group, several specific entities are identified as subcategories: مرض السكري دون مضاعفات (diabetes without complications), نقص تركيز السكر (hypoglycemia) as a distinct clinical manifestation, فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). This hierarchical arrangement reflects how individual pancreatic endocrine disorders—ranging from uncomplicated diabetes to excess insulin production and glucagon‑secreting tumors—are interconnected with each other and with more general endocrine disease categories, supporting a comprehensive approach to diagnosis, assessment, and patient management.
